retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Summarize the appropriate evaluation of premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Outline the management options available for premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Identify strategies for improving care coordination and communication among the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for premalignant lesions of the endometrium. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: TP53 inactivation as a major driver of most serous carcinomas (type 2), some high-grade endometrioid carcinomas (type 1), and many uterine carcinosarcomas; and inactivation of either gene as drivers of some clear cell carcinomas. [5] [6] Mutation or inactivation of TP53 is an early event in the pathogenesis of serous tumors and is seen in nearly 75% of EICs, which precede serous carcinomas. [2] Other molecular events implicated in the pathogenesis of serous carcinoma include somatic mutations in PPP2R1A , FBXW7, SPOP, CHD4, and TAF1; amplification and/or overexpression of ERBB2, MYC, and CCNE1 (cyclin-E); and overexpression of p16 and synuclein-γ. [2] [5] The altered PI3K pathway is also detected in serous carcinomas, mainly due to mutations in PIK3CA (in 17–43% of tumors), with some showing PTEN and PIK3R1 mutation. [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Histopathology -- Endometrial Cancer Classification Systems
retrieved_docs.content: The International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics and the World Health Organization grade endometrial cancers based on nonsquamous solid architecture percentage and nuclear appearance. Generally, low-grade disease has less than 50% of solid component and absence of marked nuclear atypia, and high-grade carcinomas have greater than 50% of solid component and marked nuclear atypia. [16] Historically, endometrial cancer was divided into 2 types based on histological characteristics. Type 1 cancers are more common, typically characterized by low-grade, well-differentiated endometrial proliferation secondary to unopposed estrogen exposure. Type 2 endometrial cancers comprise poorly differentiated, aggressive serous, clear cell, or carcinosarcoma histological types. Type 2 endometrial cancers typically have a poorer prognosis. Type 1 endometrial cancers are associated with precancerous endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia lesions, whereas type 2 is associated with serous endometrial intraepithelial carcinoma. Immunohistochemical stains, including p53, phosphatase and tensin homolog, estrogen receptor, p16, napsin A/hepatocyte nuclear factor 1-beta, and AT-rich interaction domain-containing protein 1A, are performed to help identify endometrial cancer subtypes; however, these tests are frequently difficult to reproduce and have significant overlap between histologic types. [3] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Mullerian Ducts (Paramesonephric Ducts) -- Clinical Significance -- Hand-Foot-Genital Syndrome (HFG)
retrieved_docs.content: HFG is an autosomal dominant condition due to mutations in the Hoxa13 gene. Patients with this disorder have shortened thumbs, great toes, and a bicornuate or didelphys uterus. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Longstanding molecular observations implicate PTEN inactivation, and it is seen in over 70% of the lesions. Thus, PTEN appears to be an early driver of atypical endometrial hyperplasia. [7] The systematic sequencing of endometrioid carcinomas has identified over 200 protein-encoding genes as pathogenic driver genes for type 1 EC. [3] Overall, there is dysregulation of the PI3K–PTEN–AKT–mTOR, RAS–MEK–ERK, and WNT–β-catenin pathways; microsatellite instability (MSI), mismatch repair (MMR) defects; and a relatively high occurrence of POLE mutations associated with a rare ultra-mutated phenotype. [5] The mutation of the ARID1A tumor suppressor gene may also be seen. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Premalignant lesions of the endometrium are a characteristic sequence of atypical endometrial hyperplasia or intraepithelial neoplasia that may give rise to the two main types of endometrial carcinomas. Type 1 occurs in estrogen predominance and/or progesterone insufficiency state and resembles proliferative endometrium. Type 2 is the serous type of endometrial carcinoma normally seen with postmenopausal endometrial atrophy, where mutation of P53 leads to intraepithelial carcinoma and progression. Type 1, on the other hand, harbors the inactivation of PTEN and other pathways. The presenting symptoms for premalignant lesions are menorrhagia and metrorrhagia (type 1) and postmenopausal bleeding (type 2). Management of premalignant lesions includes hysterectomy (total hysterectomy, not supracervical hysterectomy). In selected younger patients wanting to have children, medical management with high-dose gestagen therapy with appropriate close histological monitoring may be done. Untreated, almost all premalignant lesions of endometrium would progress to invasive carcinoma over a period of a few years. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients suffering from premalignant lesions of the endometrium.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Enhancing our understanding of the premalignant stages of EC development will allow us to pursue earlier diagnosis and facilitate appropriate stratification of women at risk of developing EC, permitting timely and appropriate therapeutic interventions. This involves an interprofessional team approach. Patients will often present to their primary care provider with abnormal vaginal bleeding. These physicians, physician assistants, and nurse practitioners in obstetrics/gynecology and family medicine will be the first-line providers to assess the patient. To assist with the diagnosis, these providers will rely on pathologists to review and diagnose endometrial tissue samples.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Etiology -- Protective Factors Against Endometrial Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, coffee and tea consumption may reduce the risk of endometrial cancer. Tobacco cigarette smoking is also associated with very significant reductions in risk for endometrial cancers in both prospective and case-control studies, which show the most significant decrease of risk in heavy smokers over increasing years of use. However, tobacco use is not recommended for this protective effect. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Bicornuate Uterus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Research has shown that specific genes are involved in the development of the Müllerian ducts. These include Pax, Lim1, Emx2, Wnt4, and Wnt9b, which play a role during the early formation of these structures. [6] Other genes, such as those in the homeobox family and Wnt7a, help guide the development of the Müllerian ducts into specific reproductive organs. For example, Wnt7a helps activate the Hoxa10 and Hoxa11 genes. The Hoxa genes (Hoxa9, 10, 11, and 13) are expressed in specific regions along the ducts, and this pattern is crucial for the formation of the fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and vagina. [7] Mutations in HOXA13 have been linked to hand-foot-genital syndrome, a condition that causes abnormalities in the limbs and the reproductive tract, including issues with Müllerian duct fusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: A nested case-control study of EH progression, using extensive histopathology reports, concluded that AH was 14 times more likely to progress to endometrial carcinoma as compared to the women that presented with disordered proliferative endometrium without hyperplasia. Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia was unlikely to develop into endometrial carcinoma. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Implement appropriate screening protocols for patients at increased risk of endometrial cancer, such as those with obesity, diabetes, or Lynch syndrome. Differentiate between endometrial cancer and other gynecologic conditions presenting with similar symptoms, like fibroids or endometrial hyperplasia. Identify signs and symptoms suggestive of endometrial cancer, such as abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, or uterine enlargement. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes for patients affected by endometrial cancer. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Biopsy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the indications for endometrial biopsy. Describe the steps involved in performing an endometrial biopsy. Summarize the most common complications of an endometrial biopsy. Explain the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to ensure appropriate management of endometrial biopsy results, ensure that patients receive appropriate follow-up care, and improve patient outcomes. retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Glandular Cells (AGS) -- Clinicopathologic Correlations -- Management
retrieved_docs.content: Those with atypical endometrial cells should undergo endometrial as well as endocervical sampling. Colposcopy may be delayed if endometrial pathology is identified in these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Pathophysiology -- Pathophysiological Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: In low-grade endometrioid cancers, the most commonly occurring endometrial cancer type, malignant endometrial transformation results from mitogenic stimulation of EIN precursor lesions secondary to estrogenic stimulation. Contributing factors to this malignant transformation of EIN precursor lesions likely involve genetic mutations (eg, PTEN, ARID1A ) and stromal cells that mediate endometrial epithelial differentiation, proliferation, and interactions between β-catenin and paracrine hormonal stimuli. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Endometrial Hyperplasia
retrieved_docs.content: Type 1 endometrial carcinoma, aka endometrioid carcinoma, makes up approximately 75% of all endometrial carcinoma diagnoses. Type 1 involves a loss of PTEN, a tumor suppressor gene, which leads to genetic mutations in endometrial cells. Type 1 is related to prolonged, unexposed estrogen exposure and typically occurs in post-menopausal women ages 55 to 65. A family history of Lynch syndrome, also known as hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC), is an additional risk factor. HNPCC has an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern showing a family history of ovarian, colon, or other gastrointestinal cancers. Of importance is that these individuals are also at high risk for developing Type 1 endometrial carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone, a synthetic progestogenic compound, may be used to treat انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometrial hyperplasia). By supplying progestin activity, 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone counteracts estrogen‑driven proliferation of the uterine lining, helping to normalize endometrial thickness and lower the risk of progression to malignancy. This therapeutic application highlights the role of hormonal modulation in managing intrauterine disorders and underscores the importance of appropriate hormonal therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1790
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are situated within the uterus and are linked to اضطراب رحمي, an Arabic term for uterine disorder. Endometriosis is likewise located in the uterus and is classified as a type of اضطراب رحمي, indicating that it is a specific uterine disorder. The shared uterine location and the common association with uterine disorders underscore the importance of recognizing that many pregnancy‑related complications and conditions such as endometriosis involve the uterus and fall under the broader category of uterine pathology, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient care.

Diabetic neuropathy is highlighted as a key risk factor for lower‑extremity ulcers, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring in diabetic patients. Overall, the integrated classification supports comprehensive documentation, risk assessment, and coordinated management across these interconnected medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine/endometrial disorders are classified as a subset within the broader category of enlarged uterine leiomyoma tumors (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), indicating that conditions affecting the uterus and endometrium are encompassed by the pathology of large fibroid growths. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing fibroid enlargement as a central component in the spectrum of uterine/endometrial disease and suggests that assessment of fibroid size and impact is a key aspect of managing patients with uterine/endometrial disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 537
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is linked hierarchically to both اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder) and اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder) as parent categories, indicating that disorders of the female reproductive system can be considered subtypes of broader pregnancy‑related and uterine conditions. Within this overarching category, specific diseases such as cancer of the uterus and endometriosis are included. Endometriosis is further classified as a type of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (female genital organ disorder) and also as a form of اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), reflecting its position both within the general female genital organ disorder hierarchy and the more specific uterine disorder group. The hierarchical structure therefore connects pregnancy disorders, uterine disorders, and female genital organ disorders, with Female Reproductive System Disorder, uterine cancer, and endometriosis positioned as specific manifestations within this network. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding how various female reproductive conditions relate to each other and to broader reproductive health categories, supporting more comprehensive diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with several important obstetric conditions. It is linked to تدلّي الحبل السري (cord prolapse), a rare but emergent complication in which the umbilical cord descends into the birth canal and can compromise fetal oxygenation if not promptly managed. Intrauterine pregnancy is also associated with العقم (infertility), reflecting that difficulties in achieving conception may be related to underlying reproductive pathology that can influence pregnancy outcomes. Additionally, intrauterine pregnancy is associated with الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ (endometritis), an infection of the uterine lining that can arise during or after pregnancy and may lead to maternal morbidity if untreated. Recognizing these associations helps clinicians monitor at‑risk pregnancies, intervene early in emergencies such as cord prolapse, address underlying infertility factors, and prevent or treat endometrial infection to improve maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is positioned as a parent category for other complications of pregnancy. The category other complications of pregnancy is linked to the specific disorders اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder) and اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). In addition, Female Reproductive System Disorder directly includes اضطراب الحمل and اضطراب رحمي as its own parent entities, indicating that these two disorders are subcategories within the broader Female Reproductive System Disorder classification. The “has relationship” connection between other complications of pregnancy and اضطراب رحمي further emphasizes the association of uterine disorders with other pregnancy‑related complications. This hierarchical organization clarifies how female reproductive system disorders are grouped and related, supporting clearer clinical understanding and patient‑care planning.

Diagnostic procedures—including endometrial sampling, D&C, uterine biopsy, and histopathology of genital organs—are linked to the “histopathology procedures related to genital organs and breast” SMQ and support precise disease staging and treatment planning. The entire framework emphasizes the inter‑relationship of surgical excisions, diagnostic sampling, anatomical targets (uterus, cervix, ovaries, fallopian tubes), associated malignant and benign conditions, and the clinical specialty responsible, thereby enabling standardized documentation, specialist referral, and comprehensive patient care.
